actions.reasoning: Joining holidays_events with how='inner' silently drops all non-holiday rows, reducing the dataset to only ~350 unique holiday dates and discarding the vast majority of normal trading-day sales history.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from the correct left-join CA-000011 by changing how='left' to how='inner', causing massive row loss on non-holiday dates.

actions.reasoning: The date column is stored as a string in train, test, oil, and holidays_events; parsing it to datetime is a universal first step before any calendar decomposition, join, or lag feature.
actions.context_notes: Applied to train, test, and all supplementary tables (oil, holidays_events, transactions) that have a date column. Prerequisite for DATE_PART_FEATURE, LAG_FEATURE, and date-keyed joins.

actions.reasoning: Joining oil.csv with how='inner' drops all weekend and non-trading-day rows because those dates have no oil price entry, silently shrinking the training data by roughly 30%.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from the correct left-join CA-000010 by changing how='left' to how='inner', causing roughly 30% row loss on weekends and holidays.

actions.reasoning: Joining the stores lookup table adds city, state, store type, and cluster — categorical store-level features unavailable in train.csv but used by virtually all strong baselines.
actions.context_notes: Adds: city, state, type, cluster. Keyed by store_nbr.

actions.reasoning: Computing sales lags without group_by_columns mixes lag values across different store-family combinations, introducing cross-series leakage that inflates in-sample performance and degrades generalization.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from the correct grouped lag CA-000019 by omitting group_by_columns=['store_nbr','family'], causing cross-series leakage.

actions.reasoning: Dropping the date column before temporal feature extraction destroys all calendar signals and makes lag and rolling window features impossible to compute downstream.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault that drops 'date' instead of 'id', eliminating the date column before any temporal feature work has occurred.

actions.reasoning: Oil price is a macro-economic exogenous feature strongly correlated with consumer spending patterns in Ecuador. Most competitive notebooks join it onto the main training table by date.
actions.context_notes: Adds: dcoilwtico. Left join because oil.csv has trading-day gaps (weekends/holidays have no oil price entry). Imputation of dcoilwtico should follow this join.

actions.reasoning: Filtering to keep only transferred==True rows retains the moved or cancelled holiday entries and discards actual holidays. After a holidays_events left join, it also drops ordinary non-holiday rows where transferred is missing, inverting the intent of the correct filter.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from CA-000024 by keeping transferred==True rows instead of the null-safe missing-or-False set.

actions.reasoning: Label-encoding store_nbr treats arbitrary store IDs as an ordinal numeric sequence, implying a false ordering relationship between stores that has no business meaning.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss that applies label encoding to the integer store ID rather than the meaningful family categorical, producing a misleading ordinal feature.

actions.reasoning: Zero-filling the sparse oil price series creates spurious zero-oil days on weekends and holidays, which the model will learn as a predictive signal that does not reflect actual market conditions.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of the correct interpolation CA-000013 that replaces linear interpolation with zero fill, creating fake zero-price days.

actions.reasoning: Ecuador holiday and event data is a first-class exogenous signal in this competition. Holidays cause measurable sales spikes and dips and are referenced in the official competition description.
actions.context_notes: Adds: type, locale, locale_name, description, transferred. Holiday filtering (removing transferred and Work Day rows) is typically applied to holidays_events before or after this join.
evidence_snippets: df_holiday = pd.merge(df, holiday_events, how='left', on='date', validate='many_to_one')

actions.reasoning: Joining transactions on date only (instead of [date, store_nbr]) causes a many-to-many fan-out that multiplies each row by the number of stores, producing duplicate rows and inflated transaction counts.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from the correct composite-key join CA-000012 by removing store_nbr from the join keys.

actions.reasoning: Inner-joining the stores table silently drops rows for any store ID mismatch. Using left join is always safer and is the correct approach.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from the correct left-join CA-000009 by changing how='left' to how='inner'.

actions.reasoning: Daily transaction counts capture store-level foot traffic. Some top notebooks include it as an additional input feature alongside oil prices and holiday indicators.
actions.context_notes: Adds: transactions. Keyed by composite [date, store_nbr]. Uncommon but used in stronger multi-feature baselines.

actions.reasoning: A single 30-day lag misses both the daily autocorrelation (lag 1) and the critical weekly seasonality (lag 7). Using only a monthly lag produces a weaker autoregressive signal than the canonical [1, 7] pair.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000019 that uses a coarse monthly lag instead of the informative daily and weekly lags.

actions.reasoning: Lagging the onpromotion flag instead of sales yields a promotion-lag feature rather than a sales autoregressive feature, missing the core temporal signal the model needs to learn from.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000019 that targets onpromotion instead of sales as the lag column.

actions.reasoning: Rolling mean of sales computed without group_by_columns mixes sales across all 54 stores and 33 families, producing a meaningless aggregate signal that leaks cross-series information.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault adapting the oil rolling window CA-000014 to sales without adding the required (store_nbr, family) grouping.

actions.reasoning: Oil prices have systematic gaps on weekends and holidays. Linear interpolation or forward-fill is applied after the date-keyed join to produce a complete daily oil price series for downstream features.
actions.context_notes: Must follow the oil JOIN_LOOKUP. Some notebooks interpolate oil before joining; either order achieves the same result. Uses linear interpolation or forward-fill; both are canonicalized here.

actions.reasoning: A 30-day rolling mean of oil prices over-smooths the series, losing the short-term volatility signal. The canonical window is 7 days.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000014 using a 30-day window instead of 7.

actions.reasoning: Extracting only the year discards all sub-annual seasonality (month, day-of-week, quarter) and produces a near-constant feature over most of the training period.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic impoverished variant of CA-000017 that extracts only the least informative date part.

actions.reasoning: A 7-day rolling mean smooths daily oil price volatility and provides a more stable exogenous trend signal for the model.
actions.context_notes: Applied to dcoilwtico after interpolation. Calendar-ordered (by date), not grouped by store. Output column typically named ma_oil or dcoilwtico_ma7.

actions.reasoning: Dropping the original date column after calendar decomposition prevents downstream lag and rolling window features that require the date column.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from CA-000017 by setting drop_original=True.

actions.reasoning: Filtering to keep only type == 'Holiday' discards Transfer, Additional, Bridge, and Event entries, losing a significant portion of the holiday calendar.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss filter that keeps only the base Holiday type while discarding other meaningful holiday event types.

actions.reasoning: Transferred holidays are moved to another date; keeping them at their original date introduces phantom holiday signals on non-event days. After the holidays_events left join, normal non-holiday rows have missing transferred values, so the keep predicate must preserve missing rows as well as transferred==False rows. This is equivalent to the common pre-join notebook pattern of removing transferred=True rows from holidays_events before merging.
actions.context_notes: Applied after the holidays_events left join with a null-safe keep predicate, or equivalently to the holidays_events table before a direct join. The missing-value branch preserves ordinary non-holiday training rows.

actions.reasoning: Dropping rows where dcoilwtico is null removes all weekend and holiday dates, roughly 30% of the training data. The correct approach is interpolation (CA-000013).
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault that uses listwise deletion instead of interpolation for oil price gaps.

actions.reasoning: Mean imputation of oil prices replaces all weekend/holiday gaps with a single global mean, destroying the temporal trend.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of the correct interpolation CA-000013 that uses global mean imputation instead.

actions.reasoning: Work Day entries in holidays_events mark days that are nominally holidays but where work resumes; including them as genuine holiday signals introduces noise into the model.
actions.context_notes: Applied to holidays_events, typically combined with the transferred==False filter. Several notebooks drop both at once.

actions.reasoning: Zero-imputing oil prices on gap days produces spurious zero-price days that corrupt the oil signal.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000013 using constant=0 imputation.

actions.reasoning: One-hot encoding the 54-store store_nbr column creates 54 binary features with no meaningful ordering. The correct approach is to use the joined store metadata.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss that applies one-hot encoding to the numeric store identifier rather than the meaningful family categorical.

actions.reasoning: Frequency-encoding family replaces each product family name with its row count proportion, discarding the categorical identity.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000021 using freq encoding instead of label encoding.

actions.reasoning: Calendar decomposition into year, month, day-of-week, and quarter captures seasonality and trend at multiple temporal granularities — the most common FE step in this competition.
actions.context_notes: Full calendar expansion variant. Requires PARSE_DATETIME to have run first. dayofweek encodes weekly seasonality; quarter captures within-year patterns.

actions.reasoning: Dropping store_nbr and family removes the two primary grouping keys, eliminating the ability to build per-series lag features.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault that drops the key grouping columns instead of the id row identifier.

actions.reasoning: Standard-scaling the target sales for a tree-based model is unnecessary as tree splits are scale-invariant and RMSLE already handles scale via log transform.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor representing reflexive target normalization inapplicable in a tree-model/RMSLE context.

actions.reasoning: Compact calendar variant using only month and day-of-week as the minimal temporal signals needed for seasonality modeling without redundant year or quarter features.
actions.context_notes: Alternative (OR) to the full year/month/dayofweek/quarter expansion. Used by notebooks that prefer parsimony.

actions.reasoning: Log-transforming the sales target before training causes the model to minimize log(log(sales+1)) since RMSLE already applies a log transform at evaluation time. Double-logging distorts the objective.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault representing the common mistake of manually log-transforming the target when the evaluation metric (RMSLE) already applies log internally.

actions.reasoning: Winsorizing the sales target clips extreme promotional spikes and holiday surges that are real signals, degrading the model's ability to predict high-sales events.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor representing outlier clipping of the target variable, which suppresses real sales peaks.

actions.reasoning: Aggregating sales mean by family only ignores the store dimension, averaging across all 54 stores and losing store-level variation.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor applying a groupby-agg over only the family dimension, collapsing across stores.

actions.reasoning: Lag-1 (previous day) and lag-7 (same day last week) sales are the most common autoregressive features in this competition, capturing both daily continuity and weekly seasonality.
actions.context_notes: Must be computed within each (store_nbr, family) group to avoid cross-series leakage. lag_periods [1, 7] is the most common pair; some notebooks extend to 14, 16, or 30 days.
evidence_snippets: lags = [1, 7, 30]; for i in lags: data_[f'sales_lag_{i}'] = grouped_data['sales'].transform(lambda x: x.shift(i))
f'{name}_lag_{i}': ts.shift(i) for i in range(lead_time, lags + lead_time)

actions.reasoning: Forward-fill only propagates the last known oil price but leaves NaN at the start of the series and does not interpolate between two known prices.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000013 using only forward-fill without the linear interpolation step.

actions.reasoning: Quantile-binning the sales target converts the regression problem into a coarse ordinal bucket, incompatible with the RMSLE regression objective.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor that discretizes the continuous regression target.

actions.reasoning: Longer-horizon lags (14 or 16 days) capture bi-weekly pay cycle patterns common in Ecuador, where paydays on the 15th and end-of-month affect consumer spending.
actions.context_notes: Alternative (OR) to the lag [1, 7] pair, or combined with it. Some notebooks use lag 16 specifically to align with Ecuador bi-weekly pay periods.
evidence_snippets: if days == 7: utl.df_info(df, '7 days lags added')

actions.reasoning: Dropping dcoilwtico after joining and interpolating it discards the main exogenous economic feature used by top notebooks.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault that drops the oil price column after it has been joined.

actions.reasoning: A 7-day rolling mean of sales without careful lag alignment introduces target leakage: the window includes that date's own sales value, unavailable at inference time.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault representing a rolling sales feature that lacks the necessary lag offset to avoid using the current-period target value.

actions.reasoning: The family column (33 product categories) is a high-cardinality categorical that most tree-based and neural baselines encode as an integer label before modeling.
actions.context_notes: Alternative (OR) to one-hot encoding of family. Label encoding is more common in gradient-boosted tree notebooks (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost).

actions.reasoning: Label-encoding city produces an ordinal integer mapping for 22 cities with no geographic ordering meaning.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss applying label encoding to the city column rather than the family product categorical.

actions.reasoning: The sales column has no missing values; creating a missing indicator produces an all-zero feature with no signal.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor representing a reflexive missing-indicator step applied to a column with no nulls.

actions.reasoning: Min-max scaling oil prices compresses the series to [0, 1] and erases the absolute price level. Tree-based models are scale-invariant.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor representing reflexive feature scaling applied to oil price, inapplicable for tree-based forecasting.

actions.reasoning: Some notebooks one-hot encode the family column rather than label-encoding, producing 33 binary indicator features for each product category.
actions.context_notes: Alternative (OR) to label encoding. Less common in practice due to the 33-way expansion, but used in linear and hybrid model notebooks.

actions.reasoning: Dropping the transactions column immediately after joining it discards the daily store foot-traffic signal.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault that joins transactions then immediately drops the added column.

actions.reasoning: A lag-1-only feature misses the critical weekly seasonality (lag 7), producing a substantially weaker autoregressive signal.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic impoverished variant of CA-000019 that uses only lag 1, omitting the 7-day weekly cycle lag.

actions.reasoning: Summing sales across all product families per store collapses the family dimension, producing a store-level total that loses all product-category signal.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault aggregating sales across the family dimension.

actions.reasoning: The id column is a submission row identifier with no predictive value and must be removed from the feature matrix before training.
actions.context_notes: Very commonly done early in preprocessing. In some notebooks also combined with dropping store_nbr and date after feature extraction.

actions.reasoning: Attempting to parse store_nbr (an integer store identifier) as a datetime column will raise an exception or produce NaT values.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic fault derived from CA-000008 by applying the parse datetime operation to the wrong column.

actions.reasoning: Filtering to keep only positive sales drops valid zero-sales observations, biasing the training set and degrading RMSLE performance on zero-sales test rows.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic distractor that removes zero-sales rows, biasing the training distribution.

actions.reasoning: Back-fill only propagates the next known oil price backward and cannot fill trailing NaN values, producing a step-function approximation rather than smooth interpolation.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000013 using only back-fill without the linear interpolation step.

actions.reasoning: Median imputation replaces oil price gaps with a global median, ignoring the temporal trend and producing the same step-function artifact as mean imputation.
actions.derivation_reasoning: Synthetic near-miss of CA-000013 using global median imputation.
